speaker: interviewer
text: And we're speaking with Mrs. Sonia Zyroff. I am Marcia Blitstein, and this is Bloomingdale, Illinois, USA. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Mrs. Zyroff, we were talking earlier about when the ghetto was set up. What do you remember about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So when they took out the young men, that was right after they came in. That was '41-- June, July, something like that. Later August. But in 1941-- '41 or '40-- '41, they picked one part of the city near a swampy water, kind of must be a lake, and took out all the people. Made evacuate if they were not Jews. And they made a ghetto. And they did that '41 during the Jewish holidays. They always blend whatever they had to do was during Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. So they shoveled us all into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was lucky because, see, the Germans wanted to take out from the ghetto the [GERMAN] to be able to go out. [GERMAN] means the capable Jews. They had something they can work and do something. While I went to school and I happen to have a paper. At my time, people just from generation to generation were in a field. But I had from school that I can sew and design. So I had a permission. They opened places outside the ghetto you could go freely to work, because you were wearing-- I was wearing the yellow badge, eight centimeters across. And one in the front and one in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had a green band on my arm. I think it was the [GERMAN] or something or the swastika. I don't even remember. But that was the indication that I can work out freely from the ghetto, that the police cannot touch me. Because the ghetto was guarded with the Ukrainians all the way around watching the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I must tell you a little incident that I'll never forget. That one time-- see, there was no stores. Was nothing. Nobody could buy nothing. A Ukrainian lady came to the ghetto walls and pushed through the wires a chicken. A Jewish woman took it and gave her a piece of fabric for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody spotted that. And-- because when they put us in the ghetto, I want to say earlier that day, they organized a Judenraete. They picked a few Jewish men who were responsible, taking the orders, and they simply had to carry out the orders. So somebody spotted that situation. And that was just one of the incidents in the beginning that I will never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the woman who gave the piece of fabric and took the chicken, her husband was already killed, not with her. She was left with three or four children in the ghetto and simply starving. So, anyway, they found out about it and they gave orders to the Judenraete to bring that woman back to the Germans that she'd got the chicken. So it happened to be a woman not far from me, and that woman was hiding. And they were looking for her, looking. They couldn't find her, so they gave order to the Judenraete to bring out 25 other Jews to be killed unless they bring that woman. So the Jewish Judenraete had to find her. They knew where she was hiding. To save lives of other 25 people, and deliver that woman. So they found her hiding under a bed. And that woman and the children were screaming so that you can hear it. If there was a God, he should have heard their screaming. And they took that woman out and they killed her for that. So that was the first shock when we came to the ghetto. That's incredible. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In the ghetto, where did you live? An apartment? A flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They had several families in one building. I had one room of a big house. Just one room. Just an entrance. They pushed them together much as they could. And it was in the swampy area in the worst part of the city. And there all the Jews got in there with barbed wire really high and police guarding. Ukrainian police. We hardly saw a German. They brought the Ukrainian volunteers.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were the worst in the world. I have to say that, and I'm afraid I will forget. Lots of times when I do talk and I tell the students in the classes that the people who came here after the war, who were they who came in mass groups? The Jews and the Jew killers. So that they'd know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's a fact. I can be responsible for it. Because a Ukrainian who sat still and was kind didn't have to run. He had his home and his family. Only the Jew killers-- and I'll probably repeat myself all through the story-- that this, all of them are here. In case I forget to tell you. Canada, here mainly. And I had an experience when I went back with my son. I'll have to tell you that story later. I got on a plane chartered by Ukrainian Nazis while they made an independent Ukraine. They are free. The killers can go back now. Under the Russians, they couldn't go back. Now they heroes of the country. They liberated their country from the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of any of the Ukrainian guards? Were any of them familiar to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis? The killers? It wouldn't help you any. They changed all their names. It's nothing. Joseph [INAUDIBLE], one who my parents fed when he-- during the war and sat on the horse. I'll never forget. And there was a soft command. I knew who the leaders were in the area. I knew. Most of them, when I went back, they not there. They not there. Nobody knows. They someplace in America, South America, living up, having a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when this was going on, they were familiar faces to you? You knew some of these guards? Other than this boy that you had spoken about earlier? When you were in the ghetto. When you were in the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in the ghetto, I saw him a lot because-- I didn't see the Nazis, I saw the Ukrainian people, and they were telling me everything. Because I was going out of the ghetto every day. I left the baby with my mother, my mother and father in the ghetto, and I went to work. And I didn't suffer during the ghetto either because I was wearing always a big, loose coat, and the people who knew me. I didn't do the sewing. I was taking in the material-- was a sewing shop they opened up. All the good tailors and dress makers went to the shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was too young, so I was taking in the fabrics and taking the measurements and tell them, explaining them what they want to have done. So every one of them who came in wanted their work done right, and they knew we were suffering in the ghetto. So they'll always wrap something. Either pound of butter, a loaf of bread, or a bottle of milk, the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was always coming with a big, loose coat. And the police themselves came in have some sewing done. So they didn't watch me that close. So I used to bring in in the ghetto a lot of food. And, in fact, one time, there was a woman who ran away who was on her way to Israel. And she got caught in the middle. Her luggage went to Israel. She's from a different city and she came back and they throw her in our ghetto. And she was really starving to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some people came to me to tell me that. So I used to bring her all kinds of food. And this story is connected with another story. One of my dreams that I'll have to tell you later. So she died in the ghetto, a young woman. Yeah. You talk about the food that you were given from the Ukrainians on the outside, from the locals. What food were you given in the ghetto in terms of rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. So much inches. So much bread, weighing. I was telling one of the Americans, and she's-- telling how little it was. She says, oh, that's nice. That's what I want when I go on a diet. [LAUGHS] It made me laugh. So that we ate that much she thought it was good because when she goes on a diet, that's what she does.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remembered exactly at the beginning it was not enough to live, and too much to die. Let's put it that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't have that problem. I brought in. My parents had enough to eat, and I was helping others with bringing in some of the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents were there, you were there. Who else from your family was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was there. One of the-- two brothers were there. One of them, his wife died. One had a wife and three children in the ghetto. And the other ones, his wife died and her daughter died, and then little boy was killed. And he is-- one of them who ran away-- and I'll tell you another story after-- he was hiding-- how we bumped into each other during the night. He ran a different way. My sister ran. My father and mother ran out of the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] See, because we lived in that village. So we knew some people. So we ran, all of us. And we all knew who was Nazi, who was not. So we just knocked on doors, those who were not Nazis, and they did help us. Some were hiding us for weeks and months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you say that you were from that village. So you were familiar with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people there that were not from your village? Were there people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We the only family living there in that village. Only Jewish family. It was mainly farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the ghetto, were there people-- were there Jews of other areas that were brought to that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the whole area. See, they shoveled us into the ghetto. The Jews that lived in smaller places, yes. They brought them from everywhere to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that point in the ghetto, did you know what was going on with the war? Were you receiving any word from people or from newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were completely cut off. But I had the opportunity while I was outside. So people were coming in. One time a minister or a priest, I don't remember. A very religious man dressed himself a very civilian. And he came in especially to tell me. And he said to me, run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Run. Run, because they're going to kill you. So I said, why? We were so naive. Why? So I told him. I says, I have a baby. What shall I do with the baby? I can't run. I'm with a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was scared, maybe somebody's listening, and he walked out. So when I came back and told the people that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I must tell you something that comes to my mind too. So a few of us that was left was a good friend of mine. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. He belonged to same [NON-ENGLISH] organization and was another person and I. While we were in the ghetto, in my apartment, we discussed what to do. So we decided we knew what was coming. So we decided to get ourselves kerosene, a lot of matches, and sharp knives and axes. At least the first German or Ukrainian who will open our door to kill us, let's at least kill one. So little we know that in the same building were living in smaller rooms with other families, they were listening in what's going on in that room. Jewish people. And those Jewish people in those extra rooms were digging from their apartment deep down holes, and cover the floor up later. And prepared some food and water. And they thought when they come to look for them, they will be hidden down there. So when they heard our plans, they broke the door of my room, stormed in. And they said to us, with the axe, we are going to kill you right now if this is what you want to do. See? So they didn't-- they think--

speaker: interviewee
text: But, anyway, we had no choice. I couldn't do what we wanted to do. So a friend of mine and I just decided to run. So he ran to the woods, and I ran to the village. They captured him in the woods, and he got killed. He was a good friend of mine. And I am here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rest of your family, when you ran from the ghetto, what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see When I ran out-- my running out is such a complicated story. If you have that time to listen, how I manage. But my baby was with my parents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know if I really want to tell you how that happened. How my baby got killed. So they were in the bunker with some Jewish people. I never told that before. Just makes me-- so when I talk, I don't talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the baby was crying. She was hungry. So they dumped-- they put her head in a ditch. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she was laying killed. And my father was telling me that when he saw the baby laying there with her head of curls dead in their hiding place, my mother and him decided to walk out. Whatever happened, they have nothing to live for. They walked out the middle of the night, and they went away for hiding the same area. And they were hiding for 15 months. Pretty close to the time. I don't remember if it's exactly 15 months. And then they were captured, as I told you, when they went back for bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when exactly did you find out about your daughter? It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: When one night-- my sister ran away separately. My brother ran to the woods. We always-- we knew the farmers, who was good, who was bad. So when we came in at night, my sister and I, the farmer tells me, your mother and father are sitting in my haystack outside. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went in and we sat with them. That's when my father told me that. And I will never forget, we were sitting with my mother and father, four of us. The farmer had to feed us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was hard on them also. The times were hard. Was the war. I'll never forget the hardest part was a drink of water. Food was not that important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the farm, my mother looked out and saw a pig coming near the well there. There was special place where the pigs drink their water. So my mother said to us, I wish my neshama. You know what a neshama is? My soul would get into that pig that I can have a drink of water. So I'll never forget those words. So we sat with them, and then the farmer told us to split up because he can't afford to feed us. So my parents went to a certain friend of other one and they were hiding there. We didn't know from each other until we found out that they were captured later and killed. And I was separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then one time we come to another farmer. And he said to us, your brother and another girl are sitting. They had in their farm in the fields, the farmers dig a hole and bury potatoes for the winter in the farm under the-- you know, we'll cover up. So your brother and the girl are sitting on that farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went down there and were sitting there for maybe a week or more. And the farmer was bringing us something to eat, pretending he's going to get some potatoes from over there. And we sat there until the one time he comes all frightened. And he says, the Nazis are coming to the city, to that village, to look. Everybody knew that we ran away, we were hiding. So the word went around. So the farmer-- I'll never forget, so I told to the farmer, I said, you wouldn't be responsible because we crawled in by ourselves. You don't know. So he said to me, the Germans say that the Jews smell. That the Germans can smell the Jew on the street, even if he's hidden someplace. A Jew has a special smell that the German can find us and told us to leave. So I'll never forget that. So we had to get out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We were two winters crawling in in barns. And I was very little going into the pipes and opening the door from the barn. And we were hiding the wintertime in the haystack and the food of the animals. And some farmers we showed ourselves up. They brought us something to eat and kept us a week or two. Some farmers will say, I'll give you a piece of bread and get out of here. I don't want you here. Just get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were moving from farm to farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: People only we knew, we trusted, to their farm, we went there. But that was in the wintertime. But in the summertime was very hard. So we laid in the fields. When the harvest start growing, we laying flat on our backs, the four of us. Not in one place, one far away from the other. Without a move all day long. And those longs-- and the sun was baking. You're afraid to turn, because if the farmer sees some moving in his field, he takes a gun and he shoots. It's an animal that's ruining his farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So laying there, those long days and the sun was baking without a drink or without nothing to eat. So it was a couple weeks of that. So my brother decided during the night that he knew of some kind of priest, a Ukrainian priest, personally. That he'll go and ask for some water and bread. So the way we do it is go crawl in the barn during the night and when somebody comes into the barn, you show yourself up. So my brother gets there, shows himself up. The priest or the minister, whoever it was, his wife comes in to milk the cows, and he shows himself up because they knew him. So a couple of minutes later, the priest comes with smoking tail. And he sings all the way from the house. Called the Nazis first.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, Jew, get out of my farm. In the name of Jesus Christ, serve your punishment. And singing like that all the way to the barn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother ran out. The Nazis were coming on horses, was the lucky thing. Then the farmer-- he was running with the fields with the harvest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the priest, was his farm. So he was yelling. He wouldn't let the horses get onto those fields, because the horses will damage the crop that he could not harvest it late. So they couldn't. By the time they went around, they lost track of my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came back and told us the story. That was a good friend, a religious leader. But I lived long enough, and the Russians came in. They liberated us. They arrested him without me saying anything. They arrested him and he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was killed before my brother was killed. My brother was still hiding several months later. So some churches were preaching that too. That's Jesus because they killed Jesus. That's why. Yeah. So in going from farm to farm for short periods of time, was there one place that you were able to stop for any length of time that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was your main--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. So we were going-- was four of us. So my sister and I and my brother and a girl that my brother found in the woods. A Jewish girl from a nearby city. We were the four of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one time we had to change places and go. So we walked all night, and we came to a farm where my brother knew. Little we knew that he was a Nazi leader. But the Nazi leaders--

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, in case I forget. That woman that was hiding me-- you'll have to look at that film-- that we went back to see, her father was a big Nazi leader and his daughter was hiding me afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the beginning when I ran away, I didn't tell you-- I got carried away-- how I ran out. That's another big story. If you have time to listen how I ran out of that ghetto. So when I was working in the sewing shop, right, we knew the day that they come to take us all out from over there, not to fight us. So I decided not to go, not to wait for them to come and get me. So where we had our sewing shop was outside the ghetto. And then across the roof were living non-Jews. So I crossed towards not the roof, but the attic. So I crawled through the attic and I went on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the people usually had their toilet in that-- it was like a barn, you know, attached to the house. They were keeping some-- I don't know how you'll call it-- special room. The people lived in the front, and this was in the back. So in there was their toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran away and, without shoes, quietly ran across. And I were standing there in their toilet while they came to take the other people from the sewing shop to be-- they searched the sewing shop. Some of them, they got them from the house. So little did I know that the farmer walked by, and I was barefooted. And the door had some kind of-- that much to see through. The farmer spotted my bare feet. And while I was standing-- it was the farm kind of toilet with a big hole. You know, no-- you know, the old fashioned, like the farms have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before I knew, the Nazis opened the door. And I got so blank, I fell in the toilet and I drowned in human manure. Just drowned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the farmer opened the door. I drowned. Then the farmer called the police. The farmer called the police. So the police came.

speaker: interviewee
text: His problem was he didn't want a dead Jew in his toilet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they take a farm fork to pull me out from the toilet. So I didn't know nothing anymore. Last thing I knew, I'm laying on the floor and I open my eyes, and the Nazis are around me, and I sneezed. Big sneeze. I drowned. I was full of manure. And my sneeze splashed all over their faces. And then I got conscious. And I got up and I started running. The law was you're not supposed to kill. The German made the law not supposed to kill here and there. Just catch him. They didn't want to drag dead bodies. Catch him and bring him to one place to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they couldn't shoot. They had the guns. They couldn't shoot. They have to capture me. So as soon as I gained my conscious, I opened the back door, and I locked it from the outside. And I went all the way around. And I went back in my sewing room, opened the door, and I changed clothes, whatever I had on. Put a dress on. And I moved in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a stove with a chimney. I went through the chimney, through the attic. Now, there is-- from the house, the chimney, there was a connection. A longer connection on the attic from one chimney to the other chimney for the smoke to go out. I was so little that I crawled in in this little area. I undressed myself completely naked. Completely naked. Left the clothes on the floor. And I crawled in down there. The Nazis broke up the whole roof. This way the chimney, and from the attic they tore off the chimney. They never thought of that little small area that smoke was connecting. And I was laying there. I was almost unconscious until I felt every little brick from the chimney was in my-- started hurting me. So I pulled out little by little, these little things. And I crawled out of that hole, because they broke that chimney that goes out. So I crawled out. And I was sitting on the attic. And it started already. And I have a dream that my dead husband tells me, get up and run. They'll catch you. Just a vision. I don't know what--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got up, went down to the sewing shop, put on a dress, and walked down. Was outside the ghetto. And walked. Real early in the morning, just daylight started coming. When I'm outside the city already, I saw the Ukrainian Nazis coming in from the villages on bicycles.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't walk with the street. I walked with the fields. Just with the fields. And there was a river. Right close to the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: So several bicycle stopped with their looking binoculars, because there was orders. Three days no person should get out of the house, the non-Jews. They have to be. But this was already out of the city. So those Nazis going, looking, I pretended. I bended out, pretending I'm washing something in the river. And there were swamps. They couldn't get on the bike to come near me. And they left me and then I started wandering.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came across, was across to that village where I was born. I knew the area. And I went to somebody I knew right there and then. She had maybe 10 barrels of water changing before she could see a person there. That's how I was covered up, and with that kind of smell and everything. But, still, to let me in the house, she was afraid, so I had to get on going. She gave me something to eat. And a piece of bread to get along. And then when my wandering started in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have--
